claims.flip_evidence_text: except in the cases of clauses (i), (ii), (iv), (v) or (vi), to the extent (and only to the extent) that the Company and its subsidiaries, taken as a whole, are disproportionately affected thereby as compared with other participants in the industries in which the Company and its subsidiaries operate (in which case only such incremental disproportionate impact may be taken into account in determining whether there has been a Material Adverse Effect)
claims.notes: Clause (iv) does exclude changes in law, but the carve-out permits disproportionate impacts on the Company relative to industry peers to be considered. The claim states an unqualified exclusion and omits this material limitation.

prompt:
You are a proxy legal annotator for a research pilot. Use only the contract excerpt, the question, the model answer, and the fixed claim list.

For each claim return:
- correctness_label: true, false, or partially_true
- load_bearing_label: yes or no
- flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string
- notes: optional short note

Contract excerpt:
“Material Adverse Effect” means any event, development, change, effect, fact, condition or occurrence that, individually or in the aggregate with all other events, developments, changes, effects, facts, conditions or occurrences, has had or would reasonably be expected to have a material adverse effect on or with respect to the business, results of operation or financial condition of the Company and its subsidiaries taken as a whole, provided that no events, developments, changes, effects, facts, conditions or occurrences relating to, arising out of or in connection with or resulting from any of the following shall be deemed, either alone or in combination, to constitute or contribute to a Material Adverse Effect: (i) general changes or developments in the economy or the financial, debt, capital, credit or securities markets, or in the regulatory, legislative or political conditions in the United States or elsewhere in the world, including as a result of changes in geopolitical conditions, (ii) general changes or developments in the industries in which the Company or its subsidiaries operate, (iii) the execution and delivery of this Agreement or the public announcement or pendency of the Merger or other transactions contemplated hereby, including any impact thereof on relationships, contractual or otherwise, with customers, lessors, suppliers, vendors, investors, lenders, partners, contractors or employees of the Company and its subsidiaries, or the performance of this Agreement and the transactions contemplated hereby, including compliance with the covenants set forth herein and any action taken or omitted to be taken by the Company at the express written request of or with the express written consent of Parent or Merger Sub (provided that this clause (iii) shall not apply to any representation or warranty set forth in Section 4.5 or compliance of the covenants set forth in Section 6.1) , (iv) changes or prospective changes in any applicable Laws or regulations or applicable accounting regulations or principles or interpretation or enforcement thereof, (v) any hurricane, cyclone, tornado, earthquake, flood, tsunami, wildfire, natural disaster, act of God, pandemic, epidemic or other comparable events or outbreak or escalation of hostilities or war (whether or not declared), military actions or any act of sabotage or terrorism, or national or international political or social conditions, (vi) COVID-19 or any Law or directive issued by a Governmental Entity providing for business closures, changes to business operations, “sheltering-in-place” or other restrictions that relate to, or arise out of, an epidemic, pandemic or disease outbreak (including the COVID-19 pandemic) or any change in such Law, directive or interpretation thereof following the date of this Agreement or the Company’s or any of its subsidiaries’ compliance therewith, (vii) any change in the price or trading volume of the Shares or the credit rating of the Company, in each case, in and of itself, (viii) any failure by the Company to meet any published analyst estimates or expectations of the Company’s revenue, earnings or other financial performance or results of operations for any period, in and of itself, or any failure by the Company to meet its internal or published projections, budgets, plans or forecasts of its revenues, earnings or other financial performance or results of operations, in and of itself and (ix) arising out of or relating to the identity of Parent or any of its Affiliates as the acquirer of the Company, including the impact thereof on the relationships, contractual or otherwise, of the Company and its subsidiaries with employees, suppliers, customers, partners, vendors or any other third Person (provided, that, for purposes of clauses (vii) and (viii), the facts, circumstances, events, developments, changes, effects or occurrences giving rise to or contributing to such change may be taken into account in determining whether there has been or will be a Material Adverse Effect to the extent such change or effect is not otherwise excluded from this definition of Material Adverse Effect); except in the cases of clauses (i), (ii), (iv), (v) or (vi), to the extent (and only to the extent) that the Company and its subsidiaries, taken as a whole, are disproportionately affected thereby as compared with other participants in the industries in which the Company and its subsidiaries operate (in which case only such incremental disproportionate impact may be taken into account in determining whether there has been a Material Adverse Effect); (Page 84)

Question:
Change in law (Y/N)

Llama answer:
Yes, a change in law is excluded from being a Material Adverse Effect, as per clause (iv) of the definition.

Canonical claims:
- claim-deabdd94c7a6: Yes, a change in law is excluded from being a Material Adverse Effect, as per clause (iv) of the definition.

message.reasoning:
The user wants me to act as a proxy legal annotator. I need to evaluate the canonical claim against the contract excerpt, question, and model answer.

 Contract excerpt: Definition of "Material Adverse Effect" (MAE). It lists exclusions (i) through (ix). Clause (iv) excludes "changes or prospective changes in any applicable Laws or regulations or applicable accounting regulations or principles or interpretation or enforcement thereof". However, there's a carve-out at the end: "except in the cases of clauses (i), (ii), (iv), (v) or (vi), to the extent (and only to the extent) that the Company and its subsidiaries, taken as a whole, are disproportionately affected thereby as compared with other participants in the industries in which the Company and its subsidiaries operate (in which case only such incremental disproportionate impact may be taken into account in determining whether there has been a Material Adverse Effect)".

 So a change in law is generally excluded, but if the company is disproportionately affected compared to industry peers, the incremental disproportionate impact CAN be taken into account.

 The question is simply: "Change in law (Y/N)". This seems to be asking whether a change in law is excluded from MAE (i.e., does not constitute MAE). The Llama answer says "Yes, a change in law is excluded from being a Material Adverse Effect, as per clause (iv) of the definition."

 The canonical claim is identical to the Llama answer.

 Is the claim fully true? It's partially true because while clause (iv) excludes changes in law, the carve-out for disproportionate impact means it's not an absolute exclusion. The claim says "is excluded" without qualification. Given the carve-out, this is an oversimplification. However, in many legal annotation contexts, if the main point is that clause (iv) provides an exclusion, it might be considered true, but the carve-out is important.

 Let me think about the labels:
 - correctness_label: true, false, or partially_true. Since the exclusion is subject to a disproportionate impact exception, saying it "is excluded" without qualification is not fully accurate. It is generally excluded, but not entirely. So "partially_true" seems appropriate. However, sometimes these tasks treat the core statement as true if the exception is not the main point. But the instruction says to use the contract excerpt. The contract clearly states an exception for clauses (i), (ii), (iv), (v), (vi). So the claim is not fully true. It should be partially_true.

 Wait, let me reconsider. The question is "Change in law (Y/N)". This is ambiguous. It likely means "Is a change in law excluded from MAE?" The answer "Yes... as per clause (iv)" is correct in spirit but misses the carve-out. In legal annotation, missing a material qualification like the disproportionate impact exception makes the answer partially true.

 - load_bearing_label: yes or no. This refers to whether the evidence text (the flip) is load-bearing, i.e., whether the correctness of the claim hinges on a specific part of the text. Since the claim references clause (iv), and the exception is in the final proviso, the flip evidence text would be the exception language. Is the claim load-bearing? The claim's truth depends on the contract text. The specific text that "bears the load" of correcting or qualifying the claim is the exception proviso. So yes, it's load-bearing because the correctness hinges on the contract language, specifically the carve-out. Actually, load_bearing_label usually means: does the claim depend on a specific piece of evidence that, if flipped/misread, would change the correctness? Or does it mean is there evidence that must be cited to support or refute it? In these annotation tasks, "load_bearing" often means that the claim makes a specific assertion about the text that can be verified by a specific span, and if that span is interpreted differently, the claim fails. More precisely, if the claim is about the contract, and we need to point to the specific text that supports or contradicts it, it's load-bearing. The flip_evidence_text would be the text that shows the claim is not fully true, i.e., the exception.

 Actually, looking at similar tasks (e.g., from LegalBench or contractNLI), "load_bearing" means whether the claim is directly verifiable from a specific span of text. If yes, you provide the flip_evidence_text (the span that supports or refutes). Here, the claim is directly about clause (iv). The exception is also in the text. The claim is "Yes, a change in law is excluded... as per clause (iv)". The text of clause (iv) supports that changes in law are excluded. The exception text qualifies it. Since the claim is making an assertion that is directly tied to the text, it is load-bearing. The "flip" evidence might be the text that shows the limitation. But what exactly is "flip_evidence_text"? It might be the text that, if flipped (i.e., if it said the opposite), would make the claim false, or it might be the evidence text that demonstrates the correctness label. In some schemas, "flip_evidence_text" is the minimal span that justifies the label. Let me assume it's the text that bears the load of the correctness determination.

 Given the claim is partially_true because of the carve-out, the flip_evidence_text should be the carve-out: "except in the cases of clauses (i), (ii), (iv), (v) or (vi), to the extent (and only to the extent) that the Company and its subsidiaries, taken as a whole, are disproportionately affected thereby as compared with other participants in the industries in which the Company and its subsidiaries operate (in which case only such incremental disproportionate impact may be taken into account in determining whether there has been a Material Adverse Effect)".

 Wait, but the claim is identical to the Llama answer. The question is just "Change in law (Y/N)". The Llama answer is simplistic. The canonical claim is the same.

 Let me decide on correctness_label. Is it true, false, or partially_true?
 - True: A change in law IS excluded by clause (iv). The exception is a partial exception. The statement "is excluded" is generally true as a baseline. But the exception is material. In strict legal annotation, omitting a material exception makes it partially true.
 - Partially_true: This seems safest and most accurate.

 load_bearing_label: yes. Because the claim's validity depends directly on the contract text (clause (iv) and the proviso).

 flip_evidence_text: The exception text. Or perhaps the entire relevant sentence including clause (iv) and the exception. The prompt says "flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string". I should provide the text that is the evidence for the flip/correction. I'll provide the carve-out text.

 notes: "Claim ignores the disproportionate impact carve-out for clause (iv) exceptions."

 Let me double-check if there's any nuance. The question is extremely terse: "Change in law (Y/N)". It might be from a dataset where the expected answer is simply "Yes" (meaning it is excluded). But the canonical claim includes the full sentence. The task is to evaluate the canonical claim, not just answer the question.

 The canonical claim: "Yes, a change in law is excluded from being a Material Adverse Effect, as per clause (iv) of the definition."

 This is a strong, unqualified statement. The contract says clause (iv) exclusions are subject to the disproportionate impact exception. Therefore, a change in law is NOT entirely excluded. It is excluded only to the extent the company is not disproportionately affected. So the claim is partially true.

 What about load-bearing? Yes, the claim is load-bearing because it cites a specific clause and makes a factual assertion about it. The evidence is in the text.

 Flip evidence text: I should include the specific text that qualifies the exclusion. That would be: "except in the cases of clauses (i), (ii), (iv), (v) or (vi), to the extent (and only to the extent) that the Company and its subsidiaries, taken as a whole, are disproportionately affected thereby as compared with other participants in the industries in which the Company and its subsidiaries operate (in which case only such incremental disproportionate impact may be taken into account in determining whether there has been a Material Adverse Effect)"

 I need to format the output. The user wants for each claim return the fields. There's only one claim.

 Let me structure the response clearly.

 correctness_label: partially_true
 load_bearing_label: yes
 flip_evidence_text: "except in the cases of clauses (i), (ii), (iv), (v) or (vi), to the extent (and only to the extent) that the Company and its subsidiaries, taken as a whole, are disproportionately affected thereby as compared with other participants in the industries in which the Company and its subsidiaries operate (in which case only such incremental disproportionate impact may be taken into account in determining whether there has been a Material Adverse Effect)"
 notes: The claim correctly identifies clause (iv) as excluding changes in law, but it is incomplete because it omits the material exception allowing disproportionate impacts to be considered.

 Wait, is it possible the correctness_label should be "true" because the question is simply asking if change in law is one of the excluded categories, and the answer is yes? The exception doesn't make the statement "is excluded" false; it just limits the scope of the exclusion. In common parlance for these Q&A tasks, if the main point is correct, it might be labeled true. However, the presence of the carve-out is significant. Let me think about how these annotations are typically done.

 If a claim says "X is excluded" and the contract says "X is excluded, except under condition Y", then the claim is partially true because it's not fully accurate. It's misleading without the exception.

 I think partially_true is the most defensible and accurate label.

 One more check: The prompt says "Use only the contract excerpt, the question, the model answer, and the fixed claim list." So I must base my judgment solely on these materials.

 Contract excerpt clearly has the carve-out.
 Claim ignores carve-out.
 Therefore: partially_true.

 Final check on flip_evidence_text: Should I include clause (iv) itself or just the exception? The "flip" suggests the text that flips the claim from true to partially true, i.e., the contradictory or qualifying evidence. So the exception is the flip evidence. I'll provide that exact text.

 Let me make sure the JSON/output format is clear. The user didn't specify JSON, but listed fields. I'll provide them in a structured way, perhaps as a list or a code block. I'll use a clear format.

 Actually, looking at the prompt: "For each claim return: - correctness_label: ... - load_bearing_label: ... - flip_evidence_text: ... - notes: ..."

 I'll return it as a structured entry.

 Let's write it out.
